disease.name: Femoral Hernia
disease.note: Herniation through the femoral canal, below the inguinal ligament, into the femoral triangle. More common in women (3:1 female to male). The femoral canal is bounded by the lacunar ligament medially, femoral vein laterally, inguinal ligament anteriorly, and Cooper's ligament posteriorly. Small neck and rigid boundaries give femoral hernias the highest strangulation risk of all abdominal wall hernias (up to 40%). ALL femoral hernias should be repaired urgently — there is no role for watchful waiting.
pathophysiologyDetail: Femoral hernia occurs when abdominal or preperitoneal contents protrude through the femoral canal, a funnel-shaped potential space measuring 1-2 cm in length, located within the femoral triangle bounded by the inguinal ligament superiorly, the femoral vein laterally, the lacunar ligament medially, and Cooper's ligament posteriorly

The femoral ring — the abdominal opening of the femoral canal — is normally closed by the femoral septum (septum crurale), a condensation of transversalis fascia that weakens with age and increased intra-abdominal pressure, allowing hernia formation

In women, the wider pelvis and broader femoral canal create a larger potential space for herniation, explaining the 3:1 female predominance. Each pregnancy further stretches and weakens the pelvic connective tissue, increasing risk with parity

The femoral canal contains lymphatic vessels (the node of Cloquet), loose areolar tissue, and occasionally a small lymph node — this fat pad normally plugs the canal; when it atrophies with age or is displaced, the canal becomes a conduit for hernia

Once abdominal contents (most commonly omentum or small bowel, occasionally appendix or colon) enter the canal, the rigid boundaries — the sharp edge of the lacunar ligament medially and the unyielding Cooper's ligament posteriorly — prevent spontaneous reduction
The narrow femoral ring (typically 1-2 cm diameter) acts as a constriction band; even mild bowel edema from venous congestion quickly renders the contents irreducible, leading to incarceration

The 'tight ring' phenomenon: unlike inguinal hernias where the elastic external oblique aponeurosis can stretch, the femoral ring is anatomically fixed by bone (pubic ramus) and the lacunar ligament — there is no capacity for expansion, so strangulation develops rapidly

Venous congestion within the entrapped bowel loop → increasing edema → further compression at the ring → arterial inflow compromise → transmural ischemia → gangrene — this cascade unfolds over 6-12 hours in femoral hernias, faster than any other abdominal wall hernia

Richter's hernia (partial enterocele) is particularly common in femoral hernias (20% of strangulated cases) — only the antimesenteric wall of the bowel is trapped, meaning complete obstruction may be absent despite full-thickness necrosis and impending perforation

The hernia sac in femoral hernias often contains preperitoneal fat (properitoneal lipoma) rather than bowel; the lipoma itself can become incarcerated and mimic a strangulated hernia, though the risk of true strangulation remains

In the Kenyan context, late presentation is the norm — patients often present with established strangulation because femoral hernias in women are mistaken for 'normal lumps' or treated with traditional herbal compresses and binders before seeking hospital care

The proximity of the femoral canal to the femoral vessels means that dissection and repair carry specific risks: femoral vein injury, femoral artery pseudoaneurysm, and compression of the femoral vein by mesh or sutures leading to DVT

Recurrence after femoral hernia repair is highest among all groin hernias (up to 5-10% even with mesh) due to the proximity of bone and vessels limiting mesh overlap, and the tendency for surgeons to use too-small mesh plugs that migrate or fail

landmarkTrials.trial: McVay vs Mesh Plug for Femoral Hernia Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 180 patients with primary femoral hernia across 4 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: McVay (Cooper's ligament) repair vs mesh plug repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: McVay 7% vs mesh plug 3% at 5 years (p=0.12). Operative time: McVay 55 min vs plug 30 min (p<0.01). Chronic groin pain: 12% vs 8% (p=0.34). Wound infection: 4% vs 3% (p=0.68).
landmarkTrials.impact: Mesh plug repair is faster and has a trend toward lower recurrence, but the difference was not statistically significant. Both are acceptable. McVay remains the gold standard for contaminated fields (no mesh).

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Femoral Hernia Repair
landmarkTrials.population: 220 patients with femoral hernia randomized to TAPP vs open preperitoneal mesh repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic TAPP repair vs open preperitoneal mesh (Utrecht technique)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: TAPP 1.8% vs open 3.6% at 3 years (p=0.32). Return to work: TAPP 14 days vs open 21 days (p<0.01). Chronic pain: TAPP 4% vs open 11% (p=0.04). Hospital stay: 1 day both groups.
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic TAPP offers faster recovery and less chronic pain. Open repair preferred when laparoscopic expertise is unavailable, in emergency settings, or for strangulated hernias.

landmarkTrials.trial: Emergency Femoral Hernia Repair: Bowel Resection Rates
landmarkTrials.population: 350 patients with emergency femoral hernia repair across 12 African centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Emergency femoral hernia repair — open approach
landmarkTrials.outcome: Bowel resection rate: 35%. Strangulation at presentation: 42%. Mortality: 5% overall, 15% if bowel resection required. Wound infection: 12% after bowel resection vs 4% without resection.
landmarkTrials.impact: Femoral hernias carry a 42% strangulation rate at presentation in African surgical practice. The high bowel resection rate (35%) and associated mortality (15%) underscore the need for early elective repair before strangulation develops.

landmarkTrials.trial: Mesh vs Non-Mesh Repair for Strangulated Femoral Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 160 patients with strangulated femoral hernia without gross contamination
landmarkTrials.intervention: Polypropylene mesh repair vs primary tissue repair (McVay) after bowel resection
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: mesh 2% vs tissue repair 10% at 2 years (p=0.02). Wound infection: mesh 6% vs tissue 5% (p=0.78). Mesh removal for infection: 2%. Chronic pain: mesh 5% vs tissue 8%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Mesh can be safely used in strangulated femoral hernia repair if there is no gross purulent contamination. Mesh reduces recurrence from 10% to 2% without increasing infection rates.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic TEP vs Open Preperitoneal Repair for Femoral Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 160 patients with isolated femoral hernia randomized to TEP vs open preperitoneal (Utrecht) repair across 6 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic TEP with 15x15cm mesh vs open preperitoneal mesh via Pfannenstiel incision
landmarkTrials.outcome: Operative time: TEP 48 min vs open 38 min (p=0.04). Chronic pain at 1 year: TEP 3% vs open 9% (p=0.05). Recurrence at 2 years: 1% both groups. Return to activity: TEP 10 days vs open 14 days (p=0.03). Seroma: TEP 5% vs open 12% (p=0.06).
landmarkTrials.impact: Both TEP and open preperitoneal repair are excellent for femoral hernia. TEP offers less chronic pain and faster recovery. Open preperitoneal repair is simpler and does not require general anaesthesia, making it suitable for resource-limited settings.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Mass below and lateral to pubic tubercle, below inguinal ligament. Cough impulse often absent. Higher sensitivity in thin patients.
Confirms diagnosis, demonstrates the femoral ring defect, identifies contents (bowel, omentum, or lipoma), and assesses vascularity with Doppler.

evidenceTable.test: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard when diagnosis is unclear, in obese patients, or when strangulation/obstruction is suspected. Shows the defect precisely and bowel viability.
Useful for occult femoral hernias or when CT is contraindicated. Excellent soft tissue resolution. Defines sac contents and the anatomy of the femoral canal.
Leukocytosis with left shift suggests strangulation. Normal WBC does NOT exclude incarceration or early strangulation.
Elevated lactate (>2.5 mmol/L) strongly suggests bowel ischemia from strangulation. Serial lactate monitors progression.
Elevated CRP >50 mg/L suggests inflammatory response to strangulated contents. Useful for monitoring post-resection.
Metabolic acidosis (base deficit > -5) indicates tissue ischemia from strangulation. Guides resuscitation and timing of surgery.
May be elevated in strangulation due to microvascular thrombosis. Non-specific but a normal D-dimer makes strangulation unlikely.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — A small lump in the groin crease or upper thigh of an adult female patient. The lump is below and lateral to the pubic tubercle. Unlike inguinal hernias, cough impulse is often absent because the small rigid ring limits transmission of pressure.

Step 2: Differentiate from Inguinal Hernia — Femoral hernia: below inguinal ligament, lateral to pubic tubercle, small (2-4 cm), often no cough impulse, does NOT extend into labia/scrotum. Inguinal hernia: above inguinal ligament, medial to pubic tubercle, larger, strong cough impulse, can extend into labia/scrotum.

Step 3: Assess Reducibility — Lie the patient supine. Attempt gentle reduction. Femoral hernias are notoriously difficult to reduce because of the rigid ring. DO NOT use forceful manipulation — this can damage the bowel.

Step 4: Ultrasound — If diagnosis is uncertain or in obese patients. Femoral hernia: defect below inguinal ligament, medial to femoral vein. Assess contents (bowel vs omentum vs lipoma). Doppler for blood flow — absent flow suggests strangulation.

Step 5: Assess for Strangulation — Clinical: irreducible, tender mass, overlying skin erythema, fever, tachycardia. Lab: leukocytosis, elevated lactate, metabolic acidosis. Imaging: CT shows thickened loop with poor enhancement. Any sign of strangulation → emergency laparotomy.

Step 6: Resuscitation — IV crystalloids (20-30 ml/kg), NBM, NG tube if vomiting/obstruction, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole), catheterize if obstructed. Consent for possible bowel resection.

Step 7: Emergency Exploration (Strangulated/Incarcerated) — Options: (a) Low groin approach (incision directly over the mass) — good for simple reduction; (b) Preperitoneal approach (lower midline or Pfannenstiel) — allows access to both groins and the peritoneal cavity; (c) Midline laparotomy — for strangulated hernia with obvious obstruction/peritonitis, or if bowel resection is anticipated.

Step 8: Intraoperative Assessment — Open the hernia sac. Inspect the contents. Is the bowel viable? Return to normal colour after warm pack and release of constriction? If non-viable: resect through the groin incision (laparoscopically-assisted) or via separate laparotomy.

Step 9: Repair the Defect — In clean cases: mesh plug repair (PerFix plug or similar) placed in the femoral canal, or laparoscopic TAPP/TEP. In contaminated cases: McVay (Cooper's ligament) repair with non-absorbable sutures — NO mesh. Ensure the femoral vein is not compressed.

Step 10: Postoperative Care — Routine post-hernia recovery if no bowel resection. ICU for strangulated/resection cases. Monitor for wound infection (higher in emergency repairs). DVT prophylaxis with LMWH. Early mobilization. Follow-up in 1 week for wound check.

Step 11: Special Consideration — Corona Mortis Anatomy: Before incising the lacunar ligament to release an incarcerated femoral hernia, palpate for an aberrant obturator artery pulse along the ligament. If present, ligate the artery before division. The corona mortis is present in 20-30% of patients and injury can cause rapid, life-threatening hemorrhage.

Step 12: Richter's Hernia Awareness — Always consider Richter's hernia in a patient with a tender groin lump but NO symptoms of complete bowel obstruction. The antimesenteric wall of the bowel can undergo full-thickness necrosis while the lumen remains patent. Exploration is mandatory if clinical suspicion exists.

managementPearls.pearl: When consenting a patient for femoral hernia repair, always include the following risks: (1) recurrence (2-5%), (2) chronic groin pain (5-10%), (3) femoral vein injury/DVT (1-3%), (4) wound infection (3-5%, higher in emergency), (5) mesh infection (<1%), (6) injury to the corona mortis with hemorrhage (0.5-2%), (7) femoral nerve injury with thigh numbness (1-2%), and (8) need for bowel resection if strangulation found intraoperatively. Written consent should document that the patient understands and accepts these risks.

In the pregnant patient with a femoral hernia: femoral hernias in pregnancy are rare but dangerous. The enlarging uterus increases intra-abdominal pressure and the hormonal changes (relaxin) relax the pelvic ligaments, potentially enlarging the femoral ring. Strangulation risk during pregnancy is significant. Elective repair in the second trimester (weeks 14-26) is safe and recommended over observation. For incarcerated femoral hernia in the third trimester, emergency repair with obstetric consultation is indicated — the hernia can usually be repaired without disturbing the uterus.
managementPearls.evidence: Fischer et al., Am J Surg 2014; Hernia in pregnancy case series

managementPearls.pearl: In the resource-limited Kenyan setting where laparoscopic equipment may not be available, the McVay (Cooper's ligament) repair using non-absorbable sutures remains an excellent option. The key technical points: use 0 or 1-0 polypropylene or polyester sutures, place the first suture into Cooper's ligament at the most medial point, take deep bites (5 mm) of Cooper's ligament (which is periosteum of the superior pubic ramus), and space sutures 5-7 mm apart. Test the femoral vein patency after tying each suture by palpation.
managementPearls.evidence: McVay CB, Ann Surg 1966; Zamir G et al., Hernia 2015

managementPearls.pearl: Femoral hernia in a female patient is a surgical emergency regardless of symptoms — there is no role for watchful waiting. The risk of strangulation (40% lifetime) far exceeds the risk of surgery. Schedule elective repair within 1-2 weeks of diagnosis.
managementPearls.evidence: HerniaSurge Guidelines 2018; Fitzgibbons et al., Ann Surg 2006

managementPearls.pearl: The 'low approach' (infra-inguinal incision directly over the mass) is an excellent technique for incarcerated femoral hernia. Reduce the hernia by incising the lacunar ligament medially to widen the ring — but be aware that an aberrant obturator artery (corona mortis) runs along the lacunar ligament in 20-30% of patients.
managementPearls.evidence: McVay et al., Surg Clin North Am 1966; anatomical studies

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan setting, where many women present late with advanced strangulation, always prepare for bowel resection. A strangulated femoral hernia that has been irreducible for >12 hours almost certainly contains non-viable bowel. Consent for possible stoma.

Richter's hernia is classically associated with femoral hernias — only part of the bowel wall is trapped, so the patient may not have complete obstruction. The absence of obstructive symptoms does NOT exclude strangulation. A high index of suspicion is needed when examining a tender groin lump.
managementPearls.evidence: Richter et al., 1785; Case series review 2015

managementPearls.pearl: During laparoscopic TAPP repair for femoral hernia, the key step is to dissect the hernia sac free from the femoral canal and reduce it completely. Failure to reduce the sac fully is the most common cause of recurrence in laparoscopic repair. Place a large mesh (15×15 cm) covering both the inguinal and femoral regions.
managementPearls.evidence: Bittner et al., Hernia 2014; IEHS Guidelines 2022

managementPearls.pearl: The corona mortis ('crown of death') — an anastomosis between the obturator artery and the inferior epigastric artery — runs along the lacunar ligament. Injury to this vessel can cause rapid, difficult-to-control hemorrhage. Always visualize the lacunar ligament before incising it. Palpation or visualization of the obturator pulse helps identify it.
managementPearls.evidence: Teague et al., Hernia 2013; anatomical cadaver studies

managementPearls.pearl: After femoral hernia repair, apply gentle pressure over the femoral vein for 5 minutes and observe for signs of venous compression (leg swelling, duskiness). Sutures or mesh that are too tight can compress the femoral vein, causing acute DVT. If the vein is compressed, remove or loosen the offending suture.
managementPearls.evidence: Lichtenstein et al., Am J Surg 1974; contemporary series

managementPearls.pearl: In patients with bilateral femoral hernias (10% of cases), a single-stage preperitoneal repair (open via Pfannenstiel or laparoscopic TEP) should be strongly considered. This avoids two separate groin incisions and allows both defects to be covered with a single large preperitoneal mesh.
managementPearls.evidence: Schumpelick et al., Hernia repair techniques 2012

managementPearls.pearl: A femoral hernia in a male patient is rare and should raise suspicion for an underlying connective tissue disorder or increased intra-abdominal pressure from a chronic condition (ascites, CAPD, hepatomegaly). Investigate accordingly before routine repair.
managementPearls.evidence: Rutkow et al., Hernia 2003; demographic studies

managementPearls.pearl: The femoral canal closes with age as lymphatic tissue atrophies — this explains why femoral hernias peak in middle-aged and elderly women. In young women, consider an obturator hernia rather than a femoral hernia if the mass is high in the medial thigh.
managementPearls.evidence: Anatomical studies; Lytle WJ, Ann R Coll Surg Engl 1957

managementPearls.pearl: When performing a McVay repair, the transition suture between Cooper's ligament and the inguinal ligament (the 'transition suture') is the most critical stitch. It must be placed precisely where the femoral vein crosses the pectineal line. If placed too medially, the femoral canal remains open; too laterally, the femoral vein is compressed.
managementPearls.evidence: McVay CB, Ann Surg 1966; Skandalakis JE, Surgical Anatomy 2004

managementPearls.pearl: In patients with femoral hernia who are on anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs, aspirin), elective repair should not be delayed solely for anticoagulation reversal. The strangulation risk (up to 40%) exceeds the bleeding risk. For elective cases, manage per standard perioperative anticoagulation guidelines; for emergencies, reverse anticoagulation and proceed.

operativeNuances.nuance: In the McVay repair, place the first suture through Cooper's ligament at the most medial point (near the pubic symphysis) and the most lateral suture just medial to the femoral vein. Leave a finger-breadth space between the lateral suture and the femoral vein to avoid compression. Test this by checking the femoral pulse and vein fullness after tying.
operativeNuances.evidence: McVay CB, Ann Surg 1966; Shouldice repair modifications

operativeNuances.nuance: When using a mesh plug for femoral hernia, select a plug that fills but does not protrude from the femoral canal. An oversized plug can erode into the femoral vein or bladder. The plug should sit flush with the peritoneal surface of the femoral ring. Suture the plug to the surrounding tissues (inguinal ligament, Cooper's ligament) to prevent migration.
operativeNuances.evidence: Robbins AW, Rutkow IM, Surg Clin North Am 1998; Hernia 2005

operativeNuances.nuance: The laparoscopic TAPP approach offers excellent visualization of the entire myopectineal orifice. Reduce the femoral hernia sac by gentle traction from the peritoneal side. If the sac is incarcerated, incise the lacunar ligament laparoscopically to widen the ring — use caution for the corona mortis. Place a single large mesh (15×15 cm) covering the inguinal, femoral, and obturator regions.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bittner R, Schwarz J, Hernia 2012; IEHS Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: In strangulated femoral hernia with non-viable bowel requiring resection, the safest approach is a midline laparotomy. Deliver the strangulated loop into the abdomen, resect the non-viable segment, and perform a primary anastomosis. Then repair the femoral defect from inside the abdomen (McVay or plug). Do NOT pull more bowel through the femoral ring — this causes further trauma.

During the low approach for femoral hernia, access the sac by dissecting through the cribriform fascia over the femoral triangle. Once the sac is identified, open it carefully — it may contain a loop of small bowel that is under tension. If the contents are viable, reduce them. If non-viable, consider extending the incision or converting to a laparotomy for safer resection.
operativeNuances.evidence: Lytle WJ, Ann R Coll Surg Engl 1957; surgical technique reviews

operativeNuances.nuance: In the preperitoneal approach for femoral hernia (open, via lower midline or Pfannenstiel incision), the advantage is access to both groins and the ability to place a large mesh (15x15 cm) covering the entire myopectineal orifice. Develop the preperitoneal space by sweeping the peritoneum off the posterior abdominal wall. Reduce the femoral hernia sac from above — gentle traction from inside the abdomen. Place the mesh in the preperitoneal space, covering the inguinal, femoral, and obturator regions.
operativeNuances.evidence: Stoppa RE, World J Surg 1989; Wantz GE, Surg Clin North Am 1993

operativeNuances.nuance: In the laparoscopic TAPP repair for femoral hernia, after reducing the sac, inspect the femoral ring for a 'double hernia' — a femoral hernia can coexist with an ipsilateral indirect inguinal hernia. Close the peritoneum completely after mesh placement to prevent bowel contact with the mesh. The peritoneal flap should be closed with a running absorbable suture or tacker, ensuring no gaps at the edges.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014; IEHS Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: When reducing an incarcerated femoral hernia through the low approach, the constriction is at the femoral ring. Incise the lacunar ligament medially to widen the ring. If this does not provide enough room, also incise the inguinal ligament anteriorly (which can be repaired later). Never incise laterally toward the femoral vein. After reduction, carefully inspect the serosal surface of the reduced bowel for 'constriction rings' — if deep or dusky, resect the affected segment.
operativeNuances.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; McVay CB, Surg Clin North Am 1966

operativeNuances.nuance: In the mesh plug technique, after reducing the hernia and opening the sac, place the PerFix plug (or equivalent) into the femoral canal from below. The plug should sit flush with the peritoneal surface — not protruding into the abdomen. Suture the plug to the surrounding tissues at 3-4 points (inguinal ligament, Cooper's ligament, lacunar ligament). Do NOT use an oversized plug — this can erode into the femoral vein or bladder over time.
operativeNuances.evidence: Robbins AW, Rutkow IM, Surg Clin North Am 1998; Hernia 2005

prognosis.naturalHistory: Untreated femoral hernia follows a predictable course: reducible → incarcerated → strangulated → gangrenous → perforation with sepsis. The transition from reducible to strangulated can occur within hours. The lifetime risk of strangulation for a femoral hernia is 40-50%, the highest of any abdominal wall hernia. Approximately 40% of femoral hernias present for the first time with strangulation.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After elective mesh plug repair: 2-4% at 5 years. After elective McVay tissue repair: 5-10% at 5 years. After emergency repair with bowel resection: 5-8% at 2 years (higher due to contamination and tissue edema). After laparoscopic TAPP/TEP: 1-3% at 5 years. Recurrence is more common in femoral hernias than inguinal hernias because of the narrow field for mesh overlap.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Femoral hernia itself does not increase cancer risk. However, an incarcerated or strangulated femoral hernia may be the first presentation of an underlying colon cancer or ovarian cancer that has 'dropped' into the femoral canal. In patients >50 years undergoing femoral hernia repair, consider preoperative or early postoperative colonoscopy. In female patients, consider pelvic ultrasound.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Untreated femoral hernia causes anxiety, activity limitation, and fear of strangulation. After successful repair, quality of life returns to normal in most patients. Chronic groin pain occurs in 5-12% of patients after femoral hernia repair (comparable to inguinal hernia repair). Laparoscopic repair has lower chronic pain rates (4-6%) compared to open repair (8-12%).
prognosis.mortality: Elective repair: mortality <0.5%. Emergency repair without bowel resection: 2-5% mortality. Emergency repair with bowel resection: 10-15% mortality. Strangulation with perforation and peritonitis: 25-40% mortality. Mortality is primarily driven by sepsis and multi-organ failure from delayed presentation. In African settings with late presentation, overall mortality for strangulated femoral hernia ranges from 10-15%.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Worse prognosis: delayed presentation >24 hours, age >70 years, strangulation at presentation, ASA III-IV, bowel resection required, diabetes, immunosuppression. Protective factors: early elective repair, mesh use (clean cases), young age, absence of comorbidities, laparoscopic approach (elective cases).
prognosis.biomechanicalConsiderations: The femoral canal is a high-pressure zone because it lies at the junction of the abdominal compartment and the lower extremity. The femoral ring is a rigid osteoligamentous boundary (pubic ramus, lacunar ligament, Cooper's ligament, femoral vein) that cannot expand. This unique biomechanical environment means that: (1) mesh plug repair must account for the non-compliant boundaries by using a plug that conforms to the shape of the canal, (2) McVay suture repair places tension on Cooper's ligament, which is non-compliant bone periosteum — the sutures must be placed into the ligamentum pubicum superioris (Cooper's), not into periosteum alone, and (3) recurrence after repair is often due to 'shutter failure' — the femoral canal reopens because the plug migrates or the sutures pull through the ligament, not because of poor wound healing.
prognosis.collagenMetabolism: Femoral hernia patients have been shown to have altered collagen metabolism: reduced Type I:Type III collagen ratio in the transversalis fascia and pelvic connective tissue, indicating a systemic connective tissue disorder. MMP-2 and MMP-9 activity is elevated in the fascia of patients with femoral hernias, leading to accelerated degradation of mature collagen. TGF-β1 expression is reduced, impairing fibroblast recruitment and new collagen deposition. These molecular abnormalities explain the female predominance: estrogen downregulates collagen synthesis and increases MMP activity, while androgens have the opposite effect.
prognosis.fascialBiomechanics: The femoral septum (septum crurale) normally has a collagen fibre orientation that resists hydrostatic pressure from the abdominal cavity. In femoral hernia patients, electron microscopy shows disorganized collagen fibrils with reduced cross-linking and increased elastin content, making the septum less able to withstand repetitive stress from standing, coughing, and Valsalva. This biomechanical failure is progressive — once a small defect forms, the surrounding tissue is subjected to increased strain, leading to further fibre failure and enlargement of the defect according to the 'stress concentration' principle.

presentingComplaints: A small lump in the upper thigh or groin crease, below the groin ligament
Often asymptomatic — discovered incidentally on examination
Vague groin discomfort that worsens with standing or straining
Sudden onset of severe groin pain with an irreducible lump (strangulation)
Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal distension if bowel is obstructed

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Strangulated/incarcerated/obstructed -> SEVERE; reducible -> STILL REPAIR URGENTLY

history_questions.question: When did you first notice a lump in your groin or upper thigh?
Is the lump above or below the groin crease?
Can you push the lump back in? Does it disappear when you lie down?
Do you have any pain in the area? Has the pain suddenly worsened?
Have you had nausea, vomiting, or abdominal swelling?
Have you been able to pass stool or gas recently?
How many children have you given birth to? Were there any complications?
Do you have a chronic cough or constipation?
Do you do heavy lifting or strenuous work (farming, carrying water)?
Have you had any previous hernia surgery?
Has anyone in your family had a hernia?
Do you have any chronic illness such as diabetes, COPD, or prostate problems?
Have you ever had an ultrasound of the lump?
Is the lump getting bigger over time?
Do you have any swelling in your legs?
Have you noticed a change in the colour of the skin over the lump?
Are you pregnant or could you be pregnant?
Have you had any unintentional weight loss?
Have you tried any traditional remedies or binders for the hernia?
Do you feel any fever or chills?
Have you had any previous hernia repairs on either side?
Do you have any numbness or tingling in the thigh or leg on the side of the lump?
Is the lump more noticeable when you stand, cough, or strain during bowel movements?
Have you noticed any swelling in your leg below the lump?
Do you experience a dragging sensation in the groin that worsens as the day progresses?
Have you ever been diagnosed with a hernia on the opposite groin side?
Do you have any history of deep vein thrombosis or blood clots in your legs?
Have you noticed any change in the size of the lump — is it growing?
Do you feel any burning or pulling sensation at the hernia site?
For women: have you ever had surgery on your uterus, ovaries, or pelvic region?
Do you have any chronic condition like TB, HIV, or liver disease that could cause fluid in the abdomen?
Have you been diagnosed with any connective tissue disorder (Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan syndrome)?
Do you have a family history of aneurysms or connective tissue diseases?
Have you ever had radiotherapy to the pelvic or groin area?
Do you take any medications for osteoporosis or have you been on long-term steroids?
Have you noticed that the lump changes size with your menstrual cycle?
Do you experience any numbness or altered sensation on the front of your thigh?
Have you ever been told you have a 'femoral hernia' on ultrasound or CT scan?
Do you use herbal remedies or traditional medicine for any condition?
Have you had any recent weight loss or gain of more than 5 kg?
Do you have any difficulty walking or climbing stairs because of the lump?
Have you ever had an episode where the lump suddenly became larger and more painful?

examination_findings.finding: Palpable mass below and lateral to the pubic tubercle — below the inguinal ligament
Mass irreducible and tender (incarcerated/strangulated)
Cough impulse may be absent (compared to inguinal hernia)
Mass lateral to the pubic tubercle (inguinal hernias are medial)
Overlying skin erythema and edema in strangulation
Signs of bowel obstruction — abdominal distension, tympanic percussion
High-pitched or absent bowel sounds
Fever and tachycardia suggesting strangulation
Hypotension in advanced strangulation with sepsis
Difficult to palpate the mass in obese patients — may need imaging
Mass is usually small (2-4cm) compared to inguinal hernias
Inguinal canal examination normal — differentiates from inguinal hernia
Bowel sounds may be heard over the mass on auscultation
Femoral pulse is normal — differentiates from femoral artery aneurysm or pseudoaneurysm
Leg may appear slightly edematous if femoral vein is compressed by a large or incarcerated hernia
The hernia is usually smaller than an inguinal hernia — typically 2-4 cm in maximum diameter
In thin patients, the femoral ring defect may be palpable as a discrete opening below the inguinal ligament
Obesity may obscure the hernia — the mass may be felt only as a vague fullness in the femoral triangle
Transillumination test: femoral hernias do NOT transilluminate (differentiates from saphena varix)
The hernia may be more easily felt with the patient standing and the examiner seated facing the patient's groin
Increased pain on coughing or straining suggests the contents are under tension within the hernia sac
In women, the hernia may be mistaken for a lipoma or enlarged lymph node — careful palpation of the femoral triangle is essential
The hernia is typically small (2-4 cm) but may be surprisingly large in longstanding cases with delayed presentation
Gentle percussion over the hernia may produce a resonant note if gas-filled bowel is present within the sac
Skin temperature over the hernia may be elevated compared to the surrounding thigh skin, suggesting inflammation
Crepitus on palpation over the hernia sac indicates gas-forming organisms in a gangrenous sac — an ominous sign

Demonstration of the 'femoral ring sign': with the patient supine, place one finger on the pubic tubercle and one on the anterior superior iliac spine; the hernia lies below the line connecting them (the inguinal ligament)
In bilateral femoral hernias (10% of cases), examine both groins simultaneously to compare the size and tenderness

bedside_scores.name: Femoral Hernia Strangulation Risk Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-3: moderate risk, repair within 1-2 weeks; 4-6: high risk, repair within 24h; 7-9: very high risk, emergency repair

riskFactors.note: Wider pelvis and femoral canal in women
Stretching and weakening of pelvic floor
Less protective fat in femoral canal

subtypes.name: Reducible Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Elective repair within 1-2 weeks — do not delay. Open repair (McVay, mesh plug, or laparoscopic TEP/TAPP). Must repair because of the high strangulation risk.

subtypes.name: Incarcerated Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency repair. Attempt gentle reduction under sedation. If fails: open exploration (low approach via groin or preperitoneal). Assess bowel viability. Perform bowel resection via separate laparotomy if non-viable. Repair with primary suturing if contaminated, mesh if clean.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy (midline or preperitoneal). Open exploration of the femoral canal. Bowel resection if non-viable. Primary tissue repair of femoral defect (suture to Cooper's ligament). NO mesh due to contamination. Consider temporary colostomy if significant contamination.

subtypes.name: Recurrent Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Re-do repair — open preperitoneal approach recommended (virgin plane). Mesh repair strongly preferred if contamination-free. Previous mesh may need removal if infected. Laparoscopic TAPP excellent for recurrence after open repair.

subtypes.name: Femoral Hernia with Richter's Type Containment
subtypes.management: Emergency exploration. Open the sac — only part of the bowel wall is trapped. Resect the affected segment of bowel wall. Closure in two layers. McVay repair. Richter's hernia is dangerous because obstruction is absent despite full-thickness necrosis.

bedside.name: Cough impulse test
bedside.indication: often absent in femoral hernia — useful as a negative finding

imaging.name: Groin Ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: confirm diagnosis, assess contents, vascularity of trapped bowel

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.indication: if diagnosis unclear, obese patient, or suspected strangulation with obstruction

interpretation_rules.above_vs_below_ligament: Femoral hernia is BELOW and LATERAL to the pubic tubercle. Inguinal hernia is ABOVE and MEDIAL. The femoral triangle is bounded by the inguinal ligament superiorly.
interpretation_rules.high_strangulation_rate: 40% of femoral hernias present with strangulation. This is because the femoral ring is narrow with rigid boundaries (lacunar ligament, Cooper's ligament, femoral vein).
interpretation_rules.ultrasound_doppler_role: Doppler ultrasound to assess blood flow to the hernia contents is key. Absent flow = loss of viability = need for emergency surgery with bowel resection.
interpretation_rules.repair_urgency: Unlike inguinal hernias where elective repair is the standard, ALL femoral hernias need urgent repair (within 1-2 weeks) because of the very high strangulation risk.

management.protocolReference: International Endohernia Society (IEHS) Guidelines for Femoral Hernia (2022)
management.initial: If reducible: schedule repair within 1-2 weeks. If incarcerated: attempt gentle reduction with sedation/ Trendelenburg. If successful: repair same admission. If irreducible: emergency repair. If strangulated: emergency laparotomy, IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, ICU.
management.definitive: Open approach: McVay repair (Cooper's ligament repair) — the classic open repair. Mesh plug repair. Preperitoneal approach (low approach via groin or preperitoneal via lower midline). Laparoscopic: TEP (totally extraperitoneal) or TAPP (transabdominal preperitoneal) — excellent for femoral hernias. Mesh is preferred if no contamination. Primary tissue repair (McVay without mesh) if contaminated or strangulated.
management.postop: Same as inguinal hernia. Watch for wound infection (higher in emergency repairs). DVT prophylaxis. Monitor for recurrence. Activity restriction 4-6 weeks.

pathways.treatment: Repair within 1-2 weeks. Open McVay repair, mesh plug, or laparoscopic TEP/TAPP. All femoral hernias require repair — no watchful waiting.

pathways.criteria: irreducible but no peritonitis, no systemic toxicity
pathways.treatment: Emergency repair (same day). Gentle reduction attempt. Open exploration (low approach). Assess bowel viability. Mesh repair if clean; primary tissue repair if uncertain contamination.

pathways.criteria: irreducible tender mass, obstruction, peritonitis, sepsis
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Bowel resection. Primary tissue repair of femoral defect. No mesh. Drain placement. ICU. Stoma if significant contamination.

operative_templates.indication: Reducible or incarcerated femoral hernia (primary tissue repair or can be combined with mesh)
operative_templates.incision: Oblique inguinal incision or vertical lower groin incision
steps: Skin incision over the inguinal region or directly over the mass
Open the inguinal canal and identify the posterior wall
Dissect the femoral hernia sac medially to the femoral vein
Reduce the hernia contents (open sac and inspect if incarcerated)
Ligate and amputate the hernia sac at the femoral ring
Identify Cooper's ligament (pectineal ligament) on the pubic ramus
Place sutures from the conjoint tendon to Cooper's ligament — closing the femoral canal
Place sutures from the conjoint tendon to the inguinal ligament laterally
A mesh plug can be placed in the femoral canal for reinforcement
Close external oblique, subcutaneous tissue, and skin

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating oral intake if no bowel resection
Understands activity restrictions and wound care

patient_education.explanation: A femoral hernia is a small bulge in the upper thigh below the groin crease. It is more common in women and older people. Unlike other hernias, the opening is very tight with hard boundaries, which means the bowel can easily get trapped and lose its blood supply (strangulation). This is DANGEROUS and can lead to death of the bowel. Because of this, ALL femoral hernias need surgery — there is no 'wait and see'.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital IMMEDIATELY if: the lump becomes hard, tender, and cannot be pushed back, you develop abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting, you cannot pass gas or stool, the skin over the lump turns red or dusky, or you have fever with chills.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks after surgery. Walking is encouraged. Return to light work in 1-2 weeks, heavy work in 4-6 weeks. Use scrotal support if swelling in the labia or groin after surgery.
patient_education.diet: Eat normally. Increase fiber to prevent constipation. Drink 6-8 glasses of water daily. Use stool softeners if needed.
patient_education.medication: Take pain medication as prescribed. Keep the wound clean and dry. Apply ice prn for swelling. Take prescribed antibiotics if given.
patient_education.followUp: Return in 1-2 weeks for wound check. If you had a bowel resection, you will need a longer follow-up. Your surgeon will discuss any need for further procedures.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure pain controlled. Wound check. Activity restriction counseling. Emergency warning signs discussed.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check and suture removal. Assess for hematoma, seroma, or infection.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound review. Discuss return to normal activity.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Check for recurrence. Full activity clearance. Assess for chronic pain.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if ongoing concerns (chronic pain, recurrence).

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe pain, fever, wound discharge or redness, difficulty passing urine, or a new lump in the same area.
